paragraphs.context: of data sources, and to allow EDA Applications to organize related data into groups according to their purpose. 2 Scope 2.1 This Specification provides a means to acquire event. exception, and trace data from semiconductor equipment through the use of a named data collection plan. This Specification defines what fonn a data collection plan takes, the meaning of its contents. and an interface for managing them that is to be supported by the equipment. 2.2 This Specification defines the behavior associated with the execution of data collection plans in the fonn of finite state machines. The fonnats for data produced as a result of executing a data collection plan are also defined, as is the interface that shall be supported by an EDA Application of data collection plan output. 2.3 This Specification defines a way for the equipment to notify users when the combination of activities on the equipment. including data acquisition, is causing the equipment to perform below supplier-defined criteria. 2.4 This Specification defines a way for users to make ad hoc, on demand requests for data from the equipment, outside of a data collection plan. NOTICE: SEMI Standards and Safety Guidelines do not purport to address all safety issues associated with their use. It assumes that Clients can be uniquely identified by a text value, and that the equipment is capable of enforcing the standard privileges defined in this Standard. Such mechanisms shall be fully specified by any implementation mapping of this Specification. 3.1.2 This Specification does not define the mechanism used for detennining how to locate EDA Applications that are to receive the events defined by this Specification. It assumes that the equipment has established a communications context with any Clients that can submit requests and has provided any context necessary to send events to those EDA Applications that need to them. Such mechanisms shall be fully specified by any implementation mapping of this Specification. 3.1.3 This Specification does not define any algorithms or heuristics to use for detecting degrading equipment performance. Such techniques are specific to the equipment's internal hardware and software architecture and wiJI very likely be unique to each class and/or model of equipment. It assumes that it is possible to detect such conditions and to identify which systems comprising the equipment are affected. 3.1.4 This Specification is an abstract model only. Stereotype Name- This Specification uses the terms 'class', 'data type', 'type', or the class stereotype name interchangeably to refer to a UM L class. 6.1.2 Built-In DCP - This Specification uses the term 'built-in' DCP to refer to pre-defined DCPs that are provided with the equipment. Built-in DCPs can be activated by any Client, but cannot be changed or deleted by any Clients. Refer to 12.1.3 for details. 6.1.3 Reporting, Buffering, Sending - This Specification uses the term 'reporting' to refer to the act of collecting event, exception, or trace data values to later be sent to an operational message recipient. The term 'buffering' is used to refer to the act of storing a data report on the equipment for some period of time before sending to an operationaJ message recipient. The term 'sending' is used to refer to the act of sending a report to an operational message recipient. 6.2 Notation 6.2.1 Unified Modeling language Notation - All class and sequence diagrams in this Specification make use of the Unified Modeling Language notation. 6.2.1.1 UML Stereotypes -This Specification uses the following stereotypes in class diagrams: <<privilege>> - Classes with this stereotype identify an abstract privilege concept that grants permission to a Client to perform an activity or operation. <<interface>> - Classes with this stereotype define a collection of operations and their semantics. paragraphs.context: Request-Reply (RR) and Fire-and-Forget (FF) semantics are used in this Specification. Request-Reply messages are messages that involve an initiator and a receiver. In a Request-Reply exchange, the initiator sends a single request message to the receiver, and the receiver sends a single reply message to that request back to the initiator. Fire-and-Forget messages are messages that involve a sender and a receiver. Ln a Fire-and-Forget exchange, the sender sends a single message to the receiver, with no associated response. 6.2.6.3 Requestor/Sender - For RR semantics, identifies the entity that makes the request; for FF semantics, identifies the entity that sends the message. Can be either 'Client'. or Equipment Server'. 6.2.6.4 Responder/Receiver - For RR semantics, identifies the entity that responds to the request; for FF semantics, identifies the entity that receives the message. Can be either 'Client', 'Consumer', or 'Equipment Server'. 6.2.7 Operation Argumem Definition Table - The table below provides an example of the tables used to list and describe arguments for interface operations defined in this Specification. As soon as disabled. 9.2.4 This Specification defines a mechanism for identifying parameters to be included in trace data collection (refer to 12.8). 9.2.5 This Specification defines a mechanism to support on-tool buffering, allowing trace reports containing many collection results to be sent off-tool at a regular interval (refer to 12.1.4.1 and 12.4.1.2). 9.2.6 Cached data is a special form of trace data collection, where the data samples are collected, cached on the equipment, and then reported in an efficient array format. This technique allows for data to be collected from the equipment and reported at much higher frequency than trace data collection. The higher frequency allows for EDA Applications that require higher data sampling frequencies. Cached data is setup using requests and reports separate from trace requests and reports. 9.3 Events 9.3.1 Events allow data acquisition recipients to determine when a subsystem, process job, sensor/actuator, or other modeled system undergoes a state transition. SEMI E134-1225 SEMI 2004, 2025 semr 9.4.3 This Specification defines a mechanism to support on-tool buffering of exceptions, allowing a periodic report of any exceptions that occurred during a specified interval to be sent off-tool (refer to 12. 1 .4.1 ). 9.5 Dara Collection Plans 9.5.1 A data collection plan assembles all of the related trace, event, and exception data that may be required for a specific EDA Application's purpose. For example, an EDA Application interested in tracking the utilization of the equipment may only be interested in specific events that mark actions the equipment takes that indicate how it is being exercised, certain exceptions that may interrupt the equipment. and no trace data. Such a data collection plan might be in effect at all times when the equipment is running. 9.5.2 A fault detection EDA Application may be interested in trace, event. and exception data for a specific processing run, and may change the collected data depending on the recipe used. Such data collection plans may only be in effect while specific jobs are in process on the equipment. Coordination of data collection plan activation with job activity on the equipment is the EDA Application's responsibility and is not specified in this Standard. 9.5.3 This Standard specifies a mechanism for Clients to define one or more data collection plans off-tool and submit those definitions to the equipment (refer to I 0.3 and 12). As a simple example, consider an application that monitors equipment utilization. Such an application may have an initialization procedure that includes determining the current state of each monitored component on the equipment. Without a mechanism for requesting this information at initialization, the application would instead have to wait for state change events from each component before it could determine their state. This may not be acceptable if the application provides a visualization of the equipment to an end user who relies on the application to call attention to problem conditions on the equipment. 9. 7 .2 This Standard specifies a mechanism for Clients to issue ad hoc requests for data in order to suppon these uses through the GetObjTypeAttributes, GetObjTypelnstancelds, and GetParameterValues. operations of the DataCollectionManager interface (refer to I 0.9 and I 0. Different data may be reported for each different exception state. If the values of any requested parameters can't be obtained or refreshed as a result of such communication failures, the equipment shall use the 'null' value or the oValue' class (declared in a Subordinate Standard to this Specification) as the reported parameter value for all such affected parameters. The equipment is not required to take any other actions to notify operational message recipients of such failures. 13.2.2. I EDA Applications should be aware that such failures may temporarily prevent the equipment from reporting requested events or exceptions altogether, and/or result in reporting 'null' or oValue' (declared in a Subordinate Standard to this Specification) for requested parameters affected by the failure. paragraphs.context: trace, and cached data buffering behavior of the DCP. SEMI E134-1225 SEMI 2004, 2025 semr 13.2.4 No181iffering - The DCP is immediately sending DataCollectionReports for each requested event and exception as they are detected. TraceReports may be individually buffered, according to the 'groupSize' setting of the DCP's TraceRequests (refer to 12.4), but are also sent in the DataCollectionReport format (refer to 15.1). This state is only active if the DataCollectionPlan attribute 'bufferTimelnSeconds' attribute is set to 0. 13.2.5 Buffering - The DCP is storing all events, exceptions. trace data, and cached data in an internal buffer in the order in which they occur. Buffered data is sent in a single DataCollectionReport (refer to 15.1) when 'bufferTimelnSeconds' expires or when the equipment's DCP buffering capacity has been reached, whichever occurs first. The equipment continues buffering new data for the next interval, sending a single DataCollectionRepon when 'bufferTimelnSeconds' expires. Each DataCollectionReport sent at the end of each interval contains only the data collected during that interval. The equipment continues this buffering/sending cycle as long as the DCP is active. This state is only active if the DataCollectionPlan attribute 'bufferTimelnSeconds' attribute is >0. 13.2.6 NorSending- The DCP is waiting for a DataCollectionReport to become ready for sending to the operational message recipient associated with the Client who activated the plan. 13.2. 7 Sending - The DCP is sending a DataCollectionReport to the operational message recipient associated with the Client that activated this DCP (refer to 15.1). 13.2.8 Mo11iroringEve111Req11ests - The DCP is actively

The equipment shall use this information as the basis for replies to the GetCurrentPerfonnanceStatus operation of the SEMI E134-1225 SEMI 2004, 2025 semr DataCollectionManager interface, and for the PerfonnanceWaming and PerformanceRestored notifications sent via the DCPConsumer interface (refer to 16.2.5). 14.1.3 The equipment shall include the following minimum set of conditions in determining the performance conditions to monitor: Impairment of the equipment's ability to properly control its components for their intended purpose. paragraphs.context: semr 15.2.2 A Subordinate Standard to this Specification is expected to identify the data type used with the specific implementation technology. 15.2.3 When time is represented as an ASCII string, it shall use the ISO 8601 format. ISO 8601 permits a variety of possible formats for the same time value, so it is necessary to establish a convention for the format used by time values in this Standard. 15.2.4 For representing time values, this Standard restricts the string representation of time to the form CCYY-MM-DDThh:mm:ss.fffffffff [-+)hh:mm. 'CC' is the century (first century starts at '00'), 'YY' is the year, 'MM' is the month, and 'DD' is the day, each represented as a two-digit number with leading zeroes as needed. For years beyond 9999, additional digits can be added to the 'CC' field. 'T' separates date information from time information. 'hh' is the hour, 'mm' are the minutes. 'ss' are the seconds, and fffffffff' are the sub-seconds, supporting up to one billionth of a second (nanosecond) precision in local time. If equipment can't measure time with full precision, it shall fill in the sub-second field with zeroes as appropriate up to the measurable precision (e.g., if time can be measured with centi-second precision, all time values would contain zeros in the third through ninth columns of the sub-second field). '(-+]hh:mm' describes the difference from Coordinated Universal Time expressed as either +hh:mm or -hh:mm. 16 DCPConsumer Interface 16.1 Figure 33 shows the interface the Equipment Server uses to communicate data collection results

paragraphs.context: for publication by full letter ballot procedures on September 7, 2021. R1-1 Overview R 1-1.1 This Related Information discusses some of the key requirements and expectations of the equipment for applications that make use of Data Collection Management services for perfonning near-real-time analysis of time series (trace) data from equipment. The essential goal is for the equipment to provide sufficient, correct, and timely contextual data to such applications in order to facilitate correct and timely analysis of trace data. RI -1.2 Typical Analysis Applicatio11 Example Equipment Job A wafers Job B wafers Conlllxt communlcalN via eventa and exceplona Figure RI-I Data CGl&Jmar Conlllxtmllllllllllied by conaumers, _....._Mt wlh lnconq p.cllla Typical Time- cries Data Analysis Example RI-1 .2.2 Operational message recipients that collect trace data from the equipment need to be able to quickly make sense of incoming data in order to determine the proper analysis and/or limits to apply. ln addition to the raw low level data values from sensors. actuators, and other devices of interest. applications need to be able to quickJy determine the executing context associated with that data. The equipment needs to be able to provide sufficient context information to the application to allow it to determine at all times: What module is the data coming from? What recipe is that module running? What step is that recipe executing? What material is in that module? What job is responsible for that material? R I -1 .2.3 In addition to the information in R I - 1 .2.2, typical operational message recipients also know the manufacturing execution context (e.g., whether this an engineering lot, an experimental run, a characterization, a rework lot, a production run, a specific product, etc.) in order to properly analyze data from the equipment. While it is the operational message recipient's responsibility to manage this manufacturing context data, it is the equipment's responsibility to provide the context information described in RI-I .2.2. RI -1 .2.4 In many cases, this context data may consist of a nontrivial amount of descriptive information. It is typically not practical for all of this contextual data to be provided with each TraceRepon sent by the equipment Trace data may also be collected at rates up to, for example, IO Hz; much more frequently than the rate at which the job-related context information changes. Because of this situation, it is normally sufficient for the equipment to communicate context changes when they happen by defining events that are generated whenever any of the context described in Rl-1 .2.2 changes (refer to Figure RI-I). SEMI E134-1225 SEMI 2004, 2025 semr R 1-1.2.5 Along with such events, the equipment makes available sufficient data with those events to completely describe the new context, as well as providing a way for Clients to query the current context at any time in an adhoc fashion. Additionally, some subset of this context data can be made available for inclusion with each TraceReport, such as the ids of the material at each location in the equipment. R 1-1.2.6 Some of this context data can be provided directly through SEMI Data Collection Management and related Specifications. Specifically, the Data Collection Management Specification requires that all parameters, events, and exceptions have both a source and a name. The source can be used to specify the physical equipment component that produces the parameter, event, or exception. This infonnation can be made known to Clients, for example, through the use of SEMI El20 (Common Equipment Model) and SEMI El25 (Equipment Self Description). However, at the time of this writing. some context data is not available in standard fonn, yet is often essential for most analysis applications. For example, SEMI E40 does not provide a means for operational message recipients to detennine which sub-recipes might be executing on each module of the equipment, and does not provide a way for operational message recipients to detennine which recipe step is currently being executed on each sub-recipe. This information is essential for fault detection applications. which need to analyze and apply limits to process data that may change depending on which recipe step is being executed on which component of the equipment. Unless and until this infonnation becomes standardized, equipment suppliers will need to provide this infonnation to applications using proprietary events, exceptions. and parameters. R 1-1.2.8 Putting the standards data together with equipment-specific data to cover the gaps, we can see how an operational message recipient can take an incoming Data Collection Management TraceReport and detennine much of the processing context. A typical example might involve the following high-level steps: Inspect the self-descriptive information available from the equipment via SEMI El25 to identify the low-level sensor/actuator and process Parameters from each SEMI E 120 equipment component that are of significance to the purpose of the analysis application (diagnostic, fault detection. etc.). SEMI E134-1225 SEMI 2004, 2025 semr Create a DataCollectionPlan that includes at least one TraceRequest at the desired frequency. The TraceRequest should include a ParameterRequest for each Parameter of interest from the previous step. Inspect the SEMI EI25 data from the equipment to determine the SEMI standard carrier management, control job, process job, and substrate tracking events, exceptions. and available Parameters that provide key material and processing status context. In the DataCollectionPlan created earlier. include an EventRequest and ExceptionRequest for each context event and exception of interest. For each EventRequest, include a ParameterRequest for each context data variable available for that event. Inspect the SEMI E l25 data from the equipment to determine the supplier-defined events and data that communicate what sub-recipes and which steps are executing on which components of the equipment, and any other supplier-specific context data of significance to the purpose of the analysis application. Since timestamps of all data are referenced to the tool, synchronization of the events and data can be correlated correctly. R 1 -1.2. R 1-1.2.11 By tracking Control and Process Job events and data, an operational message recipient can always determine to which job a given substrate at a given location belongs. R 1-1.2.12 The operational message recipient is responsible for managing the manufacturing context of each job, and so knows which analysis needs to be performed on data from material from those jobs. R 1 -1.2.13 Finally, because the operational message recipient, by association with a Client, knows what the contents of the DCP are, it is also known which physical equipment component produces each trace data variable. So, the data from a TraceReport can be correlated to a physical equipment component, for which the operational message recipient already knows the material located at that component, the recipe executing on the component, the step the recipe is executing, the job the material belongs to, and the manufacturing context for that job. Provided all of this information is provided correctly and in a timely fashion, an operational message recipient can always determine which analysis and/or control limits need to be applied to each TraceRepon. paragraphs.context: semr SEMI E138-0319 (Reapproved 0224) SPECIFICATION FOR XML SEMICONDUCTOR COMMON COMPONENTS This Standard was technically approved by the Jnfonnation & Control Global Technical Committee. This edition was approved for publication by the global Audits and Reviews Subcommittee on December 14, 2023. Available at www.scmivicws.org and ,vww.scmi.org in February 2024; originally published March 2005: previously published March 2019. 1 Purpose 1.1 This Standard is a central location for the definition of common XML elements that are not specific to a single standard. but common across multiple standards. 2 Scope 2.1 This Standard defines the representation of Error, Data Types, Data Value Types. and Unit which are considered common components. The common components are used in association with other XML documents or schemas generated as part of the XML based SEMI Standard Interface Specifications. NOTICE: SEM1 Standards and Safety Guidelines do not purport to address all safety issues associated with their use. It is the responsibility oftbe users oftbe Docwnents to establish appropriate safety and health practices. and determine the applicability of regulatory or other limitations prior to use. 3 Limitations 3.1 The purpose of this Standard is to define basic components and to allow for future additions as new common components are discovered and required in conjunction with an XML based interface. Four sets of components are defined at this time in this Standard: Error, Data Types, Data Value Types, and Unit. Future additions to this Standard may be possible when new common components are identified. 4 Referenced Standards and Documents

This document does not provide tutorial information on these subjects. 6.3 Translating UML to XML 6.3.1 This document follows the guidelines for XML as outlined in SEMI E 121, Guide for Style & Usage of XML for emiconductor Manufacturing Applications. 6.3.2 Some of the key guidelines in this specification are summarized here: Attributes of a class are generally represented as elements such that they can be easily extended by other applications. Inheritance is modeled as element extension. Compositions always tum into contained elements (or contained collections if multiples are allowed). Associations and Aggregations are modeled as contained elements or arrays wherever possible to simplify the instance model. 6.3.3 The translation of a UML class to XML is documented usmg a table format illustrated by Table I. Note that the PDE model in EMI El39 does not include services, so only attribute and association representations are addressed here. Any superclass information is addressed in the text and the diagrams. 6.4 Translation Table Column Header Descripti.o11 Attribllle or Role Name - If an attribute, the nan1e of tile attribute is placed here. This applies to PDEs that have internal PDEbodies and also to those that do not. 7.2.3.2.2 XML documents can contain white space and layout information that is independent of the XML content. So, two XML documents that are logically eqwvalent (1.e., they yield the same programmatic results) might not be identical byte for byte. There is a process for nommhzmg most of these differences. 7.2.3.2.3 The W3C recommendat10n xml-c14n (http://www.w3c.org/TR/xml-c14n) defines a method for creating a canonical form of an XML document. This canonicalization process eliminates the white space and layout information from the checksum calculation. The xml-c14n recommendation shall be applied in the following way: The checksum calculation shaJI be performed on a canonicalized form of the RaP:PDE element. paragraphs.context: avoid this potential issue (e.g., suggesting the proper value for the T3 timer). 6.2 SECS Commu11icario11 link Detail 6.2.1 This Specification addresses SECS-II [SEMI ES] based protocols for interaction between semiconductor equipment, recipe editors, and the Factory Information and Control System (FICS). 6.2.2 All ECS communication links are asymmetric. One participant must be designated as the Host' and the other must be designated as the 'Equipment.' In the RaP services as defined in SEMI E l39, this asymmetry does not exist. Most RaP services may be offered by any RaPnode. 6.2.3 To promote a proper mapping from RaP to SECS-II, this Specification assigns the role of Host or Equipment to each participant in each of the possible RaPnode communication combinations: FICS- Equipment, FJCS- Editor, and Editor - Equipment. This pecification does not define support for communication between two RaPnodes of the same type. 6.2.4 Figure I illustrates the three communication links addressed in this specification. RaP FIC services shall be offered only by a RaPnode configured as a ECS 'Host.' RaP Equipment services shall be offered only by a RaP11ode configured as a SEC 'Equipment.' The EditorNode takes both roles. When the Editor is linked to the FICS, it shall act in the role of Equipment.' When the Editor 1s linked to an Equipment, it shall act in the role of 'Host. paragraphs.context: used shall match that specified in SEMI E 139.1, unless otherwise mandated in another SEMI Standard. 6.5 TransferC011tai11er Transfer 6.5.1 Individual PDEs may be large-megabytes or even gigabytes. A TransferContainer groups multiple PDEs together for transfer. Even though compression is used on this collection of PDEs, the resulting TransferContainer may be very large. Transfer of such large entities will encounter the limitations of both SECS-I I and the underlying transport layers: SEMI E4, SECS-I and/or SEMI E37, HSMS. These limitations include a limit of ~8MB for SECS-I messages and -4GB for HSMS messages. Since the TransferComainer would map to a single data item, then the ~16MB limit for a single SEC -11 data item is also important. SEMI E139.2-1108 (Reapproved 0613) SEMI 2006, 2013 semr 6.5.2 To move beyond these inherent limitations, SECS-II provides an alternative transfer method for large data sets. TransferContainer transfer is handled using Stream 13, Data et Transfer (DST). Data Set Transfer allows a large data set to be transferred in segments that are sized to be within the l imits mentioned above. These data segments are reconstructed on the receiving end to yield the original data set. In this case, the data set is the TransferC011tai11er. 6.5.3 The process of Data et Transfer (DST) is designed to fit w ithin the normal flow of RaP messages. The first approach is that any SI 3FS Read Data Set Request that follows the transfer of the last dataset fragment return the End of Data indication in the S 13 F6 reply. R1-3 Multiple Data Formats Allowed R 1-3.1 In tream 1 3 there are several data items that have their format defined as 3() and/or 5(). This means that they may take any of several formats. In the case of S(), the fomuit may be: SO, 51, 52, or 54. Rl-3.2 Within the subset of Stream 13 messages used by RaP, the following list of data items have this 'flexible' formatting defined: HANDLE, READLN, RECLN, RTYPE. Rl-3.3 Many implementers interpret this format specification to mean that they can choose to use any of the available fomuits. While this is true for all messages sent, it is not true of received messages. Data overflow situations have occurred when a receiver of a message allots I byte for a data item (expecting a format 51 }, but instead receives a larger data item format (e.g., format 54). These protocol errors are provided for m the response message defined for each service. 8. 7 .2.4.2 Figure 4 shows an example of this protocol error construct in the context of the GetPDEDirecloryResponse message. Each RaP response message 1s constructed al the top level as a "choice" of "Result" or "Error". The content of the "Result" for each message definition corresponds to the SEMI E 139 message definition, including the application error fields. The "Error" choice shall be used for reporting all protocol specific errors. This allows protocol specific errors to be efficiently handled separately from protocol independent content. Privileges define the client's access to services and functions within a SEMI El32 capability. This specification defines privileges for use with the RaP services capability. 8.8.2 Privileges for the RaP services capability are defined based on the individual RaP services. Each RaP service, with the exception of requestTo endPDEO, corresponds to a separate privilege.
